findings.cis_control: CIS 1.5  — Enable MFA for root account
findings.remediation: Go to AWS Console → IAM → Security credentials → Activate MFA. Use a virtual MFA app like Google Authenticator.

findings.cis_control: CIS 1.7  — Eliminate use of root account
findings.remediation: Create an IAM user with appropriate permissions for daily tasks. Only use root for account-level tasks that require it.
findings.ai_explanation:
**Root Account Usage Detected**

Your cloud environment has a "master key" account — called the root account — that has unlimited access to everything, and it was used recently when it should almost never be needed for day-to-day work. If this goes unaddressed, any attacker who gains access to this account could take complete, unrestricted control of your entire cloud environment, including deleting data, running up massive costs, or locking out your entire team. Fixing this matters because limiting root account use is one of the most fundamental ways to protect your business from a catastrophic breach — routine tasks should always be performed through individual accounts with only the permissions they specifically need.

findings.detail: User security-scanner has no MFA device
findings.cis_control: CIS 1.10 — Enable MFA for all IAM users
findings.remediation: Go to IAM → Users → Select user → Security credentials → Assign MFA device. Enforce MFA using an IAM policy.
findings.ai_explanation:
**What This Means:** The cloud user account named "security-scanner" is currently protected by only a username and password, with no second layer of verification required to log in.

**What Could Go Wrong:** If an attacker obtains or guesses this account's password — through phishing, a data breach, or brute force — they would have immediate, unrestricted access to your cloud environment with nothing stopping them.

**Why It Matters:** Adding Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) means that even a stolen password is useless on its own, dramatically reducing the risk of an unauthorized takeover of this account and the damage that could follow.

findings.detail: User tina has no MFA device
findings.cis_control: CIS 1.10 — Enable MFA for all IAM users
findings.remediation: Go to IAM → Users → Select user → Security credentials → Assign MFA device. Enforce MFA using an IAM policy.
findings.ai_explanation:
**User Account Missing Extra Login Protection**

Tina's cloud account is currently protected by only a password, with no second layer of verification required to log in. If her password were ever stolen, guessed, or leaked, an attacker could gain immediate, unrestricted access to your cloud environment with nothing standing in the way. Adding multi-factor authentication (MFA) — a simple one-time code from a phone app — means that even a compromised password alone is useless to an attacker, significantly reducing the risk of an unauthorized breach.

findings.detail: User security-scanner key is 1 days old
findings.cis_control: CIS 1.14 — Rotate access keys every 90 days
findings.remediation: Go to IAM → Users → Security credentials → Create new access key, update all applications using the old key, then deactivate and delete the old key.

findings.detail: No S3 buckets exist in this account
findings.cis_control: CIS 2.1  — Ensure S3 bucket not publicly accessible
findings.remediation: Go to S3 → Select bucket → Permissions → Block public access → Enable all four block public access settings.

findings.detail: User security-scanner has appropriate policy: ReadOnlyAccess
findings.cis_control: CIS 1.16 — Ensure IAM policies not attached directly
findings.remediation: Remove AdministratorAccess from IAM users directly. Instead create an IAM group with required permissions and add users to the group.

findings.detail: No security groups expose dangerous ports to internet
findings.cis_control: CIS 5.2  — No security groups allow 0.0.0.0/0 on sensitive ports
findings.remediation: Go to EC2 → Security Groups → Edit inbound rules → Replace 0.0.0.0/0 source with specific IP ranges or security group IDs.
